1:
The witnesses agree on the act and divide over its materials. Matthew Henry reads the anointing theologically: it was God's own deed made visible, done not in a solemn assembly but privately, under a hedge or by a fountain, yet no less valid for that — Ps. 2:12 bids us kiss the Son, and Samuel's kiss is at once homage, allegiance, and a settled complacency in the choice, though it eclipsed his own family's glory. Charles Ellicott supplies the grammatical-historical detail: the vial was a narrow-necked vessel from which the oil dropped, and the kiss was a customary sign of reverential homage rather than of an affection two near-strangers could hardly yet feel. Adam Clarke and John Wesley press the modesty of the rite — a prophet poured oil, kissed, and spoke, and that was the whole of it; and both note that Saul is here made not king but nagid, a captain who goes before the people over the Lord's inheritance, a flock that is God's and not his own to rule by his own will.

The one real division concerns the oil. John Gill, following the Jewish tradition, holds it was not the sacred anointing oil of the tabernacle — reserved for the house of David — but common oil, or oil of balsam; and he draws the omen the rabbis drew, that Saul and Jehu, anointed from a brittle vial, reigned briefly, while David and Solomon, anointed from a horn, reigned long. Henry agrees it was common oil, unblessed, and reads the small brittle vial as a token that Saul's kingdom would soon be cracked and that but little of the Spirit was conferred on him. Against them Ellicott judges it was no common oil at all but the holy anointing ointment used at the consecration of priests and tabernacle, poured only on the founders of dynasties or those irregularly raised to the throne.

Beneath the dispute the voices are one on the meaning of the sign. Wesley takes the pouring to signify the gifts of God's Spirit poured out to fit a man for his office, and both he and Henry lift the whole rite toward its great antitype: these sacred unctions pointed to the Messiah, the Anointed One, the King of the church and High Priest of our profession, who was anointed with the oil of the Spirit not by measure but without measure, above all the priests and princes of the Jewish church. So the first drops fall on Saul as a pledge, and the signs of vv.2–8 will confirm to him that it is indeed the Lord, and no man, who has set him over Israel.
2:
The first of the three confirming signs sends Saul to a grave, and the witnesses read it for both its geography and its lesson. Keil and Delitzsch set the frame: these signs stand in the closest relation to the anointing, given as a pledge that the God who called Saul would accompany his undertakings with divine help and practically accredit him as his own. John Gill notes the distance — Saul had some miles yet to walk from Ramah before he reached Rachel's sepulchre near Bethlehem. Charles Ellicott ties the spot to Jer. 31:15, where Rachel is heard weeping in Ramah for her children, so that prophet and patriarch's tomb are linked in one grief; and he, like Gill and Clarke, confesses that Zelzah has never been identified — perhaps the Zela of Benjamin where Saul and Jonathan were at last buried.

The one small crux is the word Zelzah itself. Adam Clarke and Ellicott both record that the Septuagint, unable to place it, rendered the Hebrew as a verb — dancing or springing vehemently, which Ewald would soften to in great haste, referring it to the eagerness of the two men who bring Saul the news. Clarke fixes Rachel's tomb near Bethlehem from Gen. 35:19, and John Wesley explains why the sepulchre may lie on the Judah–Benjamin border, for the possessions of the two tribes ran one into the other. On the plain sense there is no quarrel: two men will meet Saul, tell him the lost asses are found, and report that his father has now turned his anxiety from the beasts to the son.

Wesley draws the moral the others only imply, and it is the memorable note of the verse: the very first place Samuel directs the new king to is a grave. There Saul must read a lecture on his own mortality; now that he has a crown in his eye he must think of the grave in which all his honour will be laid in the dust. The sign that assures him of a throne first stands him beside a tomb — a providence, Keil would add, so precisely foretold that its fulfilment could only confirm Samuel a true prophet and Saul the Lord's anointed, as v.7 will make plain.
3:
The second sign meets Saul among pilgrims, and the witnesses read it for its place, its worship, and one point of translation. Keil and Delitzsch render the landmark not the plain but the terebinth of Tabor, a tree otherwise unknown save that it stood on the road from Rachel's tomb toward Gibeah. Charles Ellicott agrees it is the terebinth or oak of Tabor and reports Ewald's guess that Tabor is a variant of Deborah, so that this was the historic tree under which Deborah, Rachel's nurse, was buried (Gen. 35:8); yet he and Keil both judge the conjecture unfounded. John Wesley holds the older reading of a plain, insisting it cannot be the plain at the foot of Mount Tabor, which lay far to the north, but some other place bearing the name.

On the pilgrimage itself the voices converge. The three men are going up to God to Bethel, and Adam Clarke reads their burdens as an offering: the three kids for sacrifice, the loaves for a thank-offering, the skin of wine for a libation, so that when the blood was poured out before the Lord they would feast on the flesh and bread. Both Clarke and Wesley recall that Bethel had been a sacred spot since Jacob's vision there (Gen. 28:19); Wesley notes the confusion of the age, when the ark stood in one place and the tabernacle in another, so that men still sacrificed at the old high place. Ellicott adds that Samuel himself used to visit Bethel as judge, drawn by the crowds of pilgrims, which is why these three are on the road.

John Gill is content to mark the stage of the journey — Saul goes forward from Zelzah and Rachel's tomb to this next appointed meeting. The debate over plain or terebinth is a matter of the Hebrew word, not of substance; the witnesses agree that the sign works by placing the newly-anointed king in the path of men going up to worship God, a fit token for one who is to govern the Lord's people. It is a quiet mercy that wherever the future king turns, he meets Israel at prayer — a providence, the witnesses hint, more eloquent than the loaves the pilgrims will press upon him in v.4.
4:
This brief verse completes the second sign — the pilgrims will greet Saul and give him two of their loaves — and the three witnesses read it for the courtesy of the East, the providence in the gift, and its quiet fitness for a king. Adam Clarke supplies the grammatical-historical background most fully. The Hebrew, he notes, is literally they will inquire of thee concerning peace: in the East such a salutation is itself a token of friendship, peace to you answered by to you be peace, and to give the greeting and receive its return is to establish goodwill on both sides. So Samuel's meaning is reassuring — when Saul meets these three men he need not fear them, for they are friends, and they will prove it not only by wishing him well but by pressing on him bread for the remaining stage of his journey.

John Gill presses the surprise of the courtesy: the men know nothing of Saul's anointing and salute him only in the common way, yet finding their hearts disposed toward a stranger they inquire after his welfare and wish him every prosperity. Keil and Delitzsch keep the sign within its series, treating the salutation and the two loaves received from the pilgrims' hands as the second of the pledges by which the Lord accredits his anointed on the very day of his calling.

The loaves themselves carry the weight of the verse. They were part of the pilgrims' own sacrificial provision, yet a portion is turned aside to Saul — the first present, as the older expositors love to observe, ever paid to Israel's first king, and paid by men who knew neither what they did nor why. Providence had put it into their hearts; and a king who must one day remember that he received bread as a gift on the road is thereby schooled in humility, and taught to be content with plain food and mindful of the poor. The three voices do not differ: the sign works by clothing an ordinary act of Eastern kindness with a divine appointment, so that even a loaf of bread becomes, on this day, a seal of the promise that God is with Saul — the assurance that v.7 will state outright.
5:
The third sign is the richest, and the witnesses spend themselves on three questions: where the hill of God stood, what a company of prophets was, and what it meant to prophesy. On the place they divide a little. John Gill argues for Geba, since by now Saul had crossed from Judah into Benjamin, near his journey's end, and a Philistine garrison is known to have stood there (1 Sam. 13:3). Charles Ellicott and Keil and Delitzsch read Gibeah of God, Saul's own city, later called Gibeah of Saul, so named for its sacred height; Adam Clarke reports the Targum's guess that it was the hill where the ark then rested, but himself sets it near Gibeah.

On the schools of the prophets the grammatical-historical voices are expansive. Ellicott makes their founding one of Samuel's greatest and most patriotic works, seminaries for the higher education of Israel that flourished through the whole monarchy and, Dean Payne Smith observed, staffed David's court with men of literary skill; he cautions that these young men were not, for the most part, prophets in the awful sense of seers who pierced the future, but students trained to noble and pure lives, some of whom became public preachers of the word. Both he and John Wesley note the care lavished on music — psaltery, tabret, pipe, and harp — Wesley taking the instruments as aids to stir the spirit in God's service, as they moved Elisha (2 Kings 3:15).

The one sustained debate is what Saul's prophesying will be. Clarke argues at length that the word need not mean prediction at all: from Abraham, called a prophet where the sense is a man of prayer (Gen. 20:7), to the priests of Baal who prophesied — that is, made loud supplication — until evening (1 Kings 18:26), the root idea is intercession and praise; not a syllable of any prediction of Saul's is recorded. Wesley agrees the term covers preaching and the singing of psalms by a peculiar impulse of the Spirit, and Gill has Saul enabled on the sudden to compose and sing psalms he had never learned. The voices do not finally quarrel: whether they call it prayer, praise, or inspired song, all deny that Saul foretold anything and agree that the Spirit, working through the ordinances of a prophetic school, was about to change a plain man into another — the transformation v.6 announces.
6:
Here the sign becomes a promise about Saul himself: the Spirit of the Lord will rush upon him, he will prophesy, and he will be turned into another man. The witnesses agree on the power and differ only on how deep the change runs. Keil and Delitzsch weigh the language most carefully. Ecstatic states, they grant, have something infectious about them, spreading of themselves as in revival meetings among people with no natural affinity for them; but here there was more than psychic contagion — the Spirit of Jehovah that showed itself in the prophets' praise was to pass over to Saul and entirely transform him. Yet Keil is precise about the limit: this is not regeneration in the Christian sense, but a change resembling it, one that affected his whole disposition and lifted him out of the narrow modes of thought and feeling in which he had lived.

John Wesley reads the same event as a divine equipping. The verb, he notes, is that the Spirit will leap or rush upon Saul; and another man means one suddenly endowed with another spirit — filled with the knowledge of divine things, and with the courage, wisdom, and magnanimity that befit his new dignity. John Gill keeps to the immediate effect: the Spirit comes as a spirit of prophecy, so that Saul can at once compose hymns of praise and sing them fitly, though he had never been trained in a prophetic school — which made the wonder the greater to those who knew him.

Read together the voices frame the great question the chapter never quite lets go: was Saul made a new creature or only a new officer? Wesley and Gill dwell on the gifts that qualified him to reign; Keil marks the disposition changed but stops short of calling it saving grace. None claims that this rushing of the Spirit secured Saul's soul, and the chapter's later course will justify their caution. What all confess is that the God who calls a man to a work will fit him for it, pouring out on the plainest husbandman the Spirit that makes him, for the task, another man — a transformation that v.9 records was accomplished the moment Saul turned to go.
7:
With the signs promised, Samuel turns Saul loose upon his calling: do as occasion serves thee, for God is with thee. The witnesses read the words as a charter of confident action under providence. Keil and Delitzsch render the Hebrew idiom exactly — do to thee what thy hand findeth, that is, act according to the circumstances — and take the occurrence of the foretold signs as the assurance that God would help Saul in all he undertook as king; the first opportunity, they note, would come with the Ammonite Nahash and the siege of Jabesh (1 Sam. 11:1). Adam Clarke turns it into an argument for Samuel's inspiration: after God has shown Saul so many precisely fulfilled signs that he is under divine guidance, let him fear nothing that belongs to his office, for God is with him.

Charles Ellicott gives the sentence its ringing, exhortatory voice: Samuel will lay on Saul no imperious directions but bids him go bravely on, do well and truly whatever his hand finds, confident that God will be his guide along the road of honour and peril he is destined to travel. John Gill makes the same point more soberly — Saul may take such measures as his own prudence directs, now that the last and greatest sign has sealed his commission.

John Wesley alone enters a needful caution, and it guards the whole chapter from misreading. Do what thy hand findeth means as thou shalt have a call and opportunity; Samuel does not license Saul to seize the kingly government before the people have owned his call, but bids him hold his mind ready to undertake any public service when he is summoned to his office. The voices thus balance liberty and restraint: Saul is free, under God, to meet each occasion as it comes, yet not to run before providence. The confidence rests wholly on the closing clause, which all four underline — God is with thee — the promise that makes bold action reverent rather than presumptuous, even as v.8 at once binds that boldness to Samuel's word at Gilgal.
8:
Against the freedom just granted, this verse sets one binding reservation: Saul must go down to Gilgal, and there wait seven days for Samuel to come and sacrifice and tell him what to do. The witnesses agree that the command reaches forward past the immediate story, and most read it as pointing to the fatal trial recorded later. John Gill observes that it was not to be obeyed at once — the people had first to meet at Mizpeh and afterward at Gilgal to renew the kingdom — but that it laid down a standing rule, or rather looked to the day, some two years after, when Saul would fail to wait the seven days and be reproved (1 Sam. 13:11). John Wesley concurs that the thing here commanded was not immediately performed, comparing 1 Sam. 13:8, where Saul is charged with the sin of not tarrying.

Charles Ellicott develops this at length and draws out its meaning. The Gilgal here foreseen is the solemn assembly before the war of independence (ch. 13); the warning, doubtless repeated more fully nearer the time, guarded one thing above all — that Saul, however great his power, must never infringe the sacred privileges of the religion of the Eternal by assuming the priestly function of sacrifice, and so weaken the faith of the people by his own example of unbelief. Adam Clarke, more narrowly, refers the seven days to Samuel's coming after the Ammonite victory to confirm the kingdom (1 Sam. 11:14).

Keil and Delitzsch attend to the grammar, weighing whether the clause continues the imperative of the previous verse or stands as a conditional — if Saul goes down to Gilgal to sacrifice, he must wait till Samuel arrives. However the syntax falls, the substance is one. The witnesses agree that this single, sharp restriction turns the whole of Saul's freedom into a probation: though a king, he is put upon his good behaviour, set under the direction of the prophet and the word of God. The greatest of men must own themselves in subjection to God; and the tragedy the commentators already glimpse is that the point at which Saul was most explicitly warned would prove the very point of his fall.
9:
The narrative now records that the promise was kept: as Saul turned to go, God gave him another heart, and all the signs came to pass that day. Keil and Delitzsch read the pregnant phrase — God changed to him another heart — as shorthand for God changed him and gave him another heart, the same transforming work promised in v.6; and they note the geography, that Saul, leaving early, could easily reach Gibeah in a day, the signs falling out precisely as foretold. John Gill marks only that this happened when Saul had turned his back to set out.

Matthew Henry gives the verse its famous portrait. Of all the signs, this inward one gave Saul the greatest satisfaction: he found that God had given him another heart. A new fire was kindled in his breast such as he had never known. The lost asses were quite out of his mind; he thought now of nothing but fighting the Philistines, redressing the grievances of Israel, making laws, administering justice, providing for the public safety. He found himself raised to a boldness and bravery he had never imagined. He had no longer the heart of a husbandman — low, narrow, concerned only with corn and cattle — but the heart of a statesman, a general, a prince.

From this Henry draws the settled maxim the witnesses share: whom God calls to any service he will make fit for it. If he advance a man to another station, he will give him another heart, to those who sincerely desire to serve him with their power. Yet the commentators keep a careful reserve. Keil has already insisted the change was not regeneration but a fitting of the disposition for a task; Henry, who elsewhere in this chapter grants that Saul among the prophets was Saul still, means here a real endowment for government, not a guarantee of the soul. The verse thus stands as the pattern of every divine equipping: God does not send men to work he has not fitted them to do, and the outward signs are less to Saul than the new heart quietly given him on the road home.
10:
The third sign alone is narrated in full, and the witnesses explain why: it caused a sensation at Gibeah, Saul's home. Keil and Delitzsch note that the writer dwells on this sign because it happened publicly, before Saul's own townsfolk — when he and his attendant reached the hill, a company of prophets met him, the Spirit of God came upon him, and he prophesied among them. John Wesley presses the same point: the fulfilment of the two earlier signs is quietly assumed, and only this one is described, because it was the most eminent — the others were transient acts passing privately between a few travellers, but this was permanent and notorious, done in solemn manner before many witnesses.

Charles Ellicott reads the timing devotionally. The coming of the Spirit upon Saul as he walked may be seen as the sequel of the divine gift of the new heart bestowed in the early morning, when he left Ramah; the changed heart was a fit home for the divine Spirit that came upon him in the eventide as he drew near his ancestral city. John Gill and Adam Clarke add little here, Clarke referring back to his long note on v.5, where he argued that Saul's prophesying was prayer and inspired praise rather than prediction.

Taken together the voices frame the movement of the day: a private anointing, a secret change of heart, and now a public, unmistakable descent of the Spirit that all Gibeah could see. Ellicott's image binds the two gifts into one — morning's new heart and evening's Spirit — so that the transformation is not a sudden seizure of an unprepared man but the crown set upon a work already begun within. The sign that most convinced the people was thus the outward sealing of an inward grace; and their astonishment at seeing Saul among the prophets, which the witnesses take up in v.11, becomes itself part of the providence. Keil's stress on the public setting and Wesley's on the solemn, notorious manner meet in a single point the commentators do not dispute: God arranged that the least deniable of the three signs should fall out before the very townsfolk who would afterward have to own Saul as their king, so that when the lot named him at Mizpeh they had already seen with their own eyes that the Spirit of God had come upon him. The wonder of the neighbours is not incidental but designed, preparing the nation to receive as its first king a man whom heaven had first so conspicuously marked.
11:
Saul among the prophets astonishes all who knew him, and the witnesses read the amazement as a measure of how strange his old life was to such company. Keil and Delitzsch put it plainly: the people's cry — What has happened to the son of Kish? Is Saul also among the prophets? — presupposes that his previous life had been altogether different from that of the disciples of the prophets. Charles Ellicott agrees that the surprise shows Gibeah was his home, and that his years had been spent in rough country pursuits rather than in the pursuit of knowledge; he need not have been wild or dissolute, only uncultured by the measure of the prophetic schools, for which, the verse shows, a real respect had already grown among the people. John Wesley compresses it: Saul was a man never instructed in, exercised in, nor inclined to these matters.

John Gill observes that many at Gibeah, his native place, must have known him personally, which sharpened the wonder. Ellicott notes that the incident recurs under changed and darker circumstances later in Saul's life (1 Sam. 19:23), and that it was probably this repetition which fixed the saying as a proverb for amazement at any man found in a place till then quite strange to him.

Ellicott, citing Wordsworth, lifts the wonder to its highest key with a New Testament parallel: Is Saul among the preachers of Christ? was the question of astonishment asked by the friends of the persecutor turned apostle (Gal. 1:23). The likeness is apt, for both cases display the same thing — grace surprising those who thought they knew a man, God laying his hand on the least likely and turning the course of a life in an hour. The witnesses do not contend; together they make the townsfolk's astonishment a testimony, an unwitting public acknowledgement that something more than Saul was now at work in Saul. Keil's spare observation and Ellicott's fuller one both bend the same way: the very words of the crowd — Is Saul also among the prophets? — measure the distance grace had carried him from the rough life they remembered, and so, without meaning to, the neighbours bear witness to the change. That astonishment is exactly what the proverb of v.12 will be made to confess.
12:
A bystander's retort — But who is their father? — fixes the proverb, and the witnesses divide gently over what the question means. Keil and Delitzsch reject the reading that it asks after the prophets' president; it means rather, is their father a prophet, then? — do these men hold the prophetic spirit by inheritance, by right of birth? The point, on this view, is that they do not: prophecy came to them as a free gift of the Lord, and the same Lord could as easily bestow it on Saul. Charles Ellicott reads it the same way, citing Von Bunsen: the prophets owe their gift of persuasive speech and holy song to no accident of birth, and so Saul too may have received it as a gift of the Eternal, not as a patrimony — a reply that teaches a great spiritual lesson. John Wesley agrees entirely: who is the father of these prophets but God? They have their inspiration not from parents or education but from God, who when he pleases can inspire Saul or any man; wonder not, but give God the glory.

Against this stands a textual variant. Adam Clarke notes that the Septuagint adds is it not Kish?, which turns the question toward Saul's own father and reads more smoothly as who is his father? Keil and Ellicott both know this reading — supported by the Septuagint, Vulgate, and Syriac — and both firmly reject the emendation from their father to his father, holding that the Hebrew text, understood of the prophets' common Father, yields the richer and truer sense.

John Gill keeps to the human scene — one of the same city and company, wondering how a man of so mean an education came into such company and such a gift. However the clause is pointed, the witnesses agree on the lesson it enshrines: prophecy, like every good gift, descends from God and is not entailed by blood, so that his freedom to raise up whom he will is precisely the marvel the proverb preserves. That the saying passed into common use, Keil and Wesley note, means Israel kept on its lips, half in jest, a real confession of the sovereignty of grace — the same sovereignty that had just, in v.9, given a husbandman another heart.
13:
When Saul had made an end of prophesying he came to the high place, and the witnesses read the moment for both the nature of his gift and the temper of his heart. John Gill, citing Procopius of Gaza, notes that the gift did not continue with Saul: like the prophesying of the seventy elders in the days of Moses, it was granted to make him respectable among the people, that when he came to be chosen king they might the more readily receive one so visibly honoured by God. The Spirit's coming was real but transient — a credential, not a permanent endowment.

Keil and Delitzsch fasten on what Saul does not do. Coming home to Bamah, where his father and uncle lived, he tells them of the asses and of Samuel, but says nothing of the kingdom of which Samuel had spoken. Keil ascribes the silence to unambitious humility, expressly rejecting the guess of Thenius and Josephus that Saul was afraid of unbelief and envy; the reticence is of a piece with his hiding among the baggage later in the chapter (v.22). Charles Ellicott reads the same visit devotionally: having spent his fervour in the hymn and in ecstatic prayer, Saul, before going to his own house, betook himself at once to the high place, conscious of the change that had passed over him and desiring solitary communion in a holy sanctuary with the God who had come so near.

John Wesley joins the two threads — Saul returned to the high place with the prophets to praise God for these wonderful favours and to beg counsel and help in the high business now before him. The voices do not differ; together they sketch a man newly filled and newly humbled, who turns first to worship and keeps his own counsel about his coming greatness. The lesson the commentators draw is the same reserve they admire throughout the chapter: the surest mark that God had indeed given Saul another heart is that his first act, with a crown in prospect, is to seek God's face, and his first silence is to hide his honour rather than parade it — a modesty v.16 will test when his uncle presses him.
14:
Saul's uncle questions him and his servant about their journey, and the witnesses spend their interest on the identity of this uncle. John Gill, following the Targum and Aquila, names him Saul's father's brother, Ner, the father of Abner; he adds Josephus's account that Saul went into the house of his kinsman Abner, whom he loved above all his relations, and that it was Abner who put these questions. Charles Ellicott presses the same identification and draws a character from it: most probably the uncle was the subsequently famous Abner. Kish, a quiet, plain man, was content that his beasts were found and his son returned safe, and asked no curious questions; not so the restless, ambitious Abner, who may already have heard from the servant of the strange honours paid to his nephew by the revered Samuel, and who, struck by the change that had passed over Saul, would not let the matter rest — tell me what Samuel said unto you.

Keil and Delitzsch keep to the exchange itself: the uncle asks where they went, and Saul answers only that, failing to find the asses, they had gone to Samuel — the frank half of a truth that withholds its greater part. Adam Clarke alone hesitates over the relationship. The Hebrew word, he observes, signifies a beloved one, a friend, the same root as the name David; it is supposed to mean uncle here, but he thinks it may rather mean some familiar friend.

The small divergence — a named uncle, Abner or Ner, against an unnamed intimate — does not touch the thrust of the verse, on which all agree. Saul is pressed, at the very door of his home, by one close to him who senses that something has happened; and the whole weight of the scene falls on what he will do with the question. The witnesses have already praised his silence about the kingdom as humility; here that silence is put under gentle pressure from a curious kinsman, and the narrative holds its breath to see whether the newly-anointed man will boast. His guarded answer, completed in v.16, becomes the proof that the change in him was inward and real.
15:
The verse is only a single pressing question — Tell me, I pray thee, what Samuel said unto you — and John Gill is the one witness who pauses over it, so the synthesis must draw its meaning from his brief note, the uncle's insistence, and the surrounding narrative. Gill reads the repeated demand as a sign of quickening suspicion. On hearing that Saul had been with Samuel, and perceiving so great an alteration in his kinsman, the uncle begins to suspect something about the kingdom — for the choice of a king was what everyone was then talking of, all Israel expecting daily to hear from Samuel who the Lord had appointed to reign.

The force of the moment lies in that public expectancy pressing upon a private secret. The nation had asked for a king and been told one would be given; Samuel's movements were watched; and here is a young man of Benjamin lately returned from the prophet, visibly changed, fresh from prophesying among the sons of the prophets in the sight of his townsfolk. The uncle is no fool: he has the servant's report of the long private interview, and he has the evidence of his own eyes in the altered bearing of Saul. His second, more urgent request is not idle curiosity but the shrewd probing of a man who feels he stands near the edge of a great matter and means to draw it out.

The verse thus sharpens the tension the previous ones raised. Samuel had bound Saul to a task and a probation; God had given him another heart; the Spirit had fallen on him publicly. Now the first test of that changed heart comes not from an enemy but from an inquisitive relative, and it comes precisely at the point of the one thing Saul has been charged to keep. The narrator lets the question stand alone, unanswered for a verse, so that the reader feels the pull of it — how easily a lesser man would have yielded the secret to a trusted kinsman. What Saul does with the pressure is reserved for v.16, where his guarded reply is read by the witnesses as the surest evidence that his new heart was real, and his modesty and self-command the first fruits of the grace that had touched him.
16:
Saul tells his uncle that the asses were found but says nothing of the kingdom, and the witnesses read his silence as the crown of his early modesty. Charles Ellicott weighs two motives. One, ingeniously suggested, is the characteristic Israelite caution — the fear of betraying prematurely an important secret. But he judges it far better to suppose that Samuel had given Saul to understand that the revelation of his future was, for the present, for him alone; in his own good time God would make his will known to Israel, and till then Saul was strictly to keep his own counsel. To have imparted the secret to anyone, and to Abner especially, would have opened the door at once to intrigue and party plotting.

John Wesley lands on the same double ground more briefly: Saul told it not in obedience to Samuel, who had obliged him to secrecy, and from an humble modesty. John Gill keeps to the plain report of Saul's answer to the question. All three treat the reticence not as evasion but as the outward proof of the inward change — the modesty and humility, as well as the wisdom, of Saul in these early days of his greatness being, as Ellicott says, remarkable, and the changed heart an acknowledged fact in him.

Ellicott, citing Wordsworth, sets the silence in noble company: as Samson in the days of his humility told not his parents of the lion he had killed (Judg. 14:6), and as Saul of Tarsus spoke not of his visions and revelations of the Lord until his enemies constrained him (2 Cor. 12:1), so this Saul held his peace about his kingdom. The witnesses do not divide; they agree that the man who could carry so great a secret home to a curious kinsman and not let it slip had been truly given another heart. The reticence that began at the high place in v.13 here proves its temper, and prepares the reader to see, at Mizpeh, a king who must be sought and drawn out because he would not thrust himself forward.
17:
Samuel summons Israel to Mizpeh to make the king's election public, and the witnesses read the assembly as a gathering held pointedly unto the Lord. John Gill explains that it was Mizpeh in Benjamin, not the Gilead town, and that the people called were the heads and elders as the nation's representatives, summoned perhaps by a herald's proclamation; and he supposes the high priest present with the Urim and Thummim, and possibly the ark, the symbol of the divine presence — for wherever God's people are solemnly gathered in his name, there the Lord is. Charles Ellicott agrees that unto the Lord signifies the mysterious Urim and Thummim had been brought there, and, quoting Ewald, stresses that Saul's mysterious interview with the seer did not alone suffice: publicly, in solemn national assembly, the Spirit of the Eternal must choose him out and make him known.

Keil and Delitzsch add the reason for the place. Samuel chose Mizpeh because it was there he had once, by prayer, won for the people a great victory over the Philistines (1 Sam. 7:5); the memory of that deliverance under the government of God hangs over the very ground on which Israel now completes its exchange of that government for a king. Matthew Henry makes this the heart of the scene: Samuel acts for God among them, and before he will produce their king he reproves them for casting off the government of a prophet and desiring that of a captain, showing how happy they had been when God ruled and delivered them.

So the witnesses set the assembly in a double light, and they do not divide over it. On the one hand it is the orderly, God-honouring means by which a whole nation shall receive its first king — the lot cast unto the Lord, the priest and perhaps the ark at hand, nothing done in a corner. On the other hand it is an assembly shadowed by rebuke, convened on the field of a former mercy to a people who have preferred an arm of flesh to the almighty arm. Henry's reading governs the whole: the same act that crowns Saul convicts Israel, and the joy of a coronation is not allowed to drown the solemn charge, ye have this day rejected your God, that v.19 will press home.
18:
Samuel opens the assembly with the voice of a prophet, Thus saith the Lord God of Israel, and recounts the Exodus deliverances, and the witnesses agree that the recital is an indictment. Keil and Delitzsch, following O. von Gerlach, explain the design: before proceeding to the election itself, Samuel once more charges the people with their sin in rejecting the God who had brought them out of Egypt and delivered them from the hand of all their oppressors, that he might show how dangerous was the way they were now taking, and how bitterly they might yet repent of what they had desired. Adam Clarke notes briefly that these words are of a piece with the earlier upbraidings when Israel first demanded a king (1 Sam. 8:7).

Charles Ellicott draws out the ingratitude most fully. Before the election, Samuel again reminds Israel of its folly in voluntarily rejecting the glorious Eternal King for an earthly sovereign. It was true, Ellicott grants, that under the present circumstances of the nation the establishment of a mortal king was needful for the development of the Hebrew power; but it was no less true that such a change in the constitution would never have been necessary had the nation not first forsaken their own Sovereign, who in time past had saved them out of far greater perils than any then threatening them. John Gill observes only that Samuel speaks in the name of the Lord, using the prophets' formula.

The witnesses do not divide; they read the verse as God's own rehearsal of his saving history, set deliberately against the people's demand. The list of deliverances — from Egypt, from the Egyptians, from all the kingdoms that oppressed them — is not comfort here but reproach, each mercy a witness that the King they are replacing had never failed them. The rhetorical weight, the commentators feel, is Samuel's mercy no less than his severity: he will give them their king, for God has granted the request, but he will not let them take him without first setting before their eyes the God they are treating as insufficient. The full charge, that in asking a king they have rejected their God, follows at once in v.19.
19:
The charge now falls plainly: ye have this day rejected your God, who himself saved you out of all your adversities, and yet you have said, Set a king over us. The witnesses read the verse as the hinge on which rebuke turns into the ordered casting of the lot. John Gill states the rejection simply — in desiring another to be set over them, the people rejected their God as their king. Keil and Delitzsch render the sentence in full and then explain the procedure: Samuel directs the assembled Israelites to present themselves before the Lord, before the altar at Mizpeh, by their tribes and thousands, and the tribe of Benjamin is taken — literally snatched out by Jehovah through the lot, as Achan was taken (Josh. 7:16).

Adam Clarke lays out the fourfold narrowing by which the man is found: first the tribe, and Benjamin was taken; then the thousand, and the division of Matri; then the family, and the family of Kish; and last the individual, and Saul the son of Kish. John Wesley supplies the reason for the whole method: Samuel puts them to choose their king by lot so that all might know God had chosen Saul — for the disposal of the lot is of the Lord — and to prevent all dispute and exception, that no tribe or family might complain of the choice.

The witnesses hold the two notes of the verse together without strain. The lot is God's own act of designation, putting the choice beyond human management so that the reluctant nation cannot cavil at its king; yet the very words that introduce the lot are words of judgment, ye have rejected your God. Samuel will give Israel a king chosen by heaven, and give him in such a way that none can gainsay the choosing — but he frames the gift as the granting of a sinful request, so that the people receive their heart's desire and their reproof in a single breath. The mechanism of the lot then runs its course through the tribes to Benjamin, and the astonishing discovery that the chosen man has hidden himself waits in v.21.
20:
The lot begins its work and the tribe of Benjamin is taken, and the two witnesses who pause here read the moment for its method and its meaning. Charles Ellicott asks how the lots were cast. The text does not say, but usually it was by throwing tablets (Josh. 18:8), and sometimes by drawing from a vessel or urn (Num. 33:54); from the Hebrew word used on this occasion, Ellicott judges the latter — the drawing from an urn — was probably the method employed. John Gill notes only that Samuel caused the tribes, in their heads and representatives, to come near to the place where the lots were cast.

John Wesley presses the deeper question the falling of the lot on Benjamin raises: why this tribe, and not Judah? His answer turns on the mood in which the monarchy is granted. The kingdom had been freely promised by God to Judah (Gen. 49:10) and was to be given to that tribe in love; but now the kingdom was in a manner forced from God, wrung from him by an ungrateful people, and given them in anger — and therefore it was conferred, for the present, upon an obscure tribe. The first king would not come from the royal line, because this first king was not the gift of favour but the concession to a demand.

The witnesses, few as they are here, do not contend, and their two emphases fit together. On the surface the verse is procedure — the solemn, public drawing that lets a whole nation watch the choice narrow from twelve tribes to one. Beneath it lies Wesley's theological reading, which keeps the whole election within the frame Samuel has just set: even the tribe on which the lot falls preaches the difference between a kingdom bestowed in love and one granted in displeasure. Benjamin, small and lately almost destroyed, is a fitting cradle for a monarchy born of the people's sin; and the narrowing lot moves on, in the verses that follow, from the tribe to the family of Matri and to the son of Kish himself in v.21.
21:
The lot narrows to the family of Matri and then to Saul the son of Kish, but when they seek him he cannot be found. Keil and Delitzsch follow the sacred machinery closely: Benjamin's family of Matri is taken, then Saul himself, and when he is sought and missing the people inquire further of the Lord. This inquiry, Keil explains, was made through the high priest by means of the Urim and Thummim — the technical expression for asking of the Lord in Num. 27:21 — for at so important a gathering the high priest would surely be present, even though the text does not expressly say so, with Samuel presiding as the prophet of the Lord.

The commentators divide only over a name and a motive. Charles Ellicott raises the puzzle of Matri, which occurs in none of the Benjamite genealogies connected with Saul's house; he cannot account for the omission and records Ewald's conjecture that the name is a corruption of Bikri (1 Chron. 7:8). On the reason for Saul's absence, Adam Clarke supposes that through modesty or fear he had secreted himself, while John Gill keeps to the process, noting that they inquired of the Lord again to learn not who the man was — the lot had shown that — but where he was to be found.

The witnesses agree on the striking shape of the scene. The lot, which none can manage or foresee, has fastened infallibly on one man; and that man, at the very moment of his elevation, is nowhere to be seen. The chosen king must be sought by a fresh word from God, as though heaven itself must overrule his hiding. Clarke's modesty or fear and Keil's picture of a solemn priestly inquiry combine into the chapter's recurring portrait of a reluctant man drawn out against his shrinking — the same humility the earlier verses praised, now become almost an obstacle that only a further oracle can remove. Ellicott's textual puzzle and Clarke's note on the motive belong to the same picture: a man whose family the genealogies scarcely record, drawn from an obscure tribe, chosen infallibly by the lot and yet shrinking from the honour into hiding. Where Saul was hidden, and how he was found, v.22 discloses.
22:
They ask the Lord where the chosen man is, and the answer comes: he hath hid himself among the stuff. The witnesses read the hiding as humility and the disclosure as God's condescension. Adam Clarke marvels at the exchange — what a continual access to God, and what condescension in his attention to every request — and takes the stuff to mean the carts and baggage the people had brought to Mizpeh. John Gill understands the further inquiry as made either by lot or by the high priest's Urim and Thummim, seeking now the hiding-place of the man the lot had already named. John Wesley agrees that they enquired by Urim, or by Samuel whose prayer procured the answer, and that Saul had hidden among the baggage from a sense of his own unworthiness.

Charles Ellicott reads the psychology most fully. Saul and Samuel alone, of all the host gathered that day, knew on whom the lot must fall; and Saul, so certain after the sealing signs that he would be designated, shrank from waiting to hear the result and concealed himself among the store-tents and waggons, so that a second divine announcement was needed to discover his hiding-place and draw him forth. Ellicott then looks ahead to the one gift that would answer the coming murmur, how shall this man save us? — Saul's towering stature, by which, in that primitive age, a man seemed worthy of rule, so that Dean Stanley notes he is remembered in Muslim tradition only as Thalut, the tall one, and in the songs of his own time as the gazelle, the pride of Israel.

The witnesses do not divide on the motive: modesty, unworthiness, a shrinking from the honour thrust upon him. What they find remarkable is the collision of that shrinking with the sovereign will. God has chosen a man who does not wish to be found, and God finds him — the reluctant king dragged into the light by the very oracle he hoped might pass him over. The scene seals the chapter's paradox, that the humility which so becomes Saul is also powerless to undo his election; and the people, once he is fetched, will see for themselves the stature that makes the choice visible, as v.23 relates.
23:
They run and fetch Saul, and when he stands among the people he is taller than any of them from the shoulders upward — and the two witnesses who treat the verse read that stature as the visible mark of the chosen king. Keil and Delitzsch describe the moment: Saul was speedily fetched and brought into the midst of the assembled people, and being a head taller than all of them, as the reader has already been told of him (1 Sam. 9:2), his very bodily stature won the favour of the people. Then Samuel presents him with the words, Behold him whom the Lord hath chosen, for there is none like him in all the nation, and the whole people shout aloud, Let the king live! John Gill adds only that they ran to fetch him, eager to see and proclaim their king.

Keil is careful to distinguish what Samuel does next — he tells the people the right of the monarchy — from the harsh right of the king that had been set before them earlier as the prerogative a despot would seize. The right of the monarchy is rather the ordinance regulating the earthly kingship within the theocracy, defining the human king's duties and rights in relation to God and the people. The distinction will matter when the witnesses come to weigh what Samuel wrote in the book at v.25.

The heart of this verse, though, is the stature, and the commentators read it in the idiom of the age. In a primitive time, when a nation's strength was measured in the reach of its champion, the man who stood head and shoulders above the crowd looked every inch a king; and Saul, drawn reluctant from the baggage, needs no throne or scaffold to be seen, for on level ground he is seen above them all. The witnesses feel the poignancy of it: the one gift that instantly commends him to the people is the outward, bodily one, the height that made him goodly. It is a true credential for that hour, yet a shallow one, and the chapter's later note of a heart God had touched, and of scoffers who ask what such a man can do, already hints that a king chosen for his shoulders will be measured, in the end, by something deeper — as Samuel's own acclamation in v.24 both celebrates and tests.
24:
Samuel presents the king with See ye him whom the Lord hath chosen? and the people cry out in acclamation, and the witnesses read the scene for the ground of the choice and the words of the shout. John Gill stresses that, the choice having been made by lot whose disposal is of the Lord, it is properly ascribed to God, so that the people cannot object; Samuel appeals to their very eyes for the goodness of it, and Gill enlists even a heathen witness, Eupolemus, who reported that Saul was made king by Samuel according to the will of God. Keil and Delitzsch again note that it was Saul's bodily stature that won the favour of the people, the none like him being, in the first place, a word about his height.

John Wesley reads the phrase the same way — none like him as to the height of his stature, which was in itself commendable in a king and some indication of great endowments of mind — and takes the people's cry, let the king live, as their formal acceptance of him and their promise of subjection. He adds the reflection that has run through the chapter: none will be losers in the end by their humility and modesty, for honour, like a shadow, follows those who flee it and flees those who pursue it — the reluctant man drawn from the baggage now hailed by a nation.

The one sharp note is Adam Clarke's philology. There is no such word as God in the famous cry, he insists, nor anywhere in the Hebrew Bible: the words rendered God save the king are simply yechi hammelech, may the king live, exactly as the French Vive le roi. The correction does not divide the witnesses so much as sharpen their agreement, for all read the acclamation as a shout for the king's long and prosperous life and reign. Beneath the pageantry the commentators keep their reserve: the people accept Saul chiefly for the stature their eyes can measure, a choice ratified by the lot yet resting, on the human side, on the most outward of grounds — a foundation that the divided reception of the following verses, and of a greater King, will try.
25:
Samuel tells the people the manner of the kingdom, writes it in a book, and lays it up before the Lord, and the witnesses divide over what this document was. John Gill and Adam Clarke lean to reading it as a repetition of the stern warning about the king's arbitrary power that Samuel had given when the people first demanded a ruler (1 Sam. 8:9). Gill, with Ben Gershom and Josephus, takes it as the power a king might claim and the punishment he might inflict, written and laid up as a testimony and witness against the people hereafter; Clarke allows it was the same matter as before, though he thinks there may also have been some compact or covenant between king and people set down in the book.

Charles Ellicott reads it very differently, as the Law of the Kingdom: the divinely established rights and duties of the God-appointed king, and, crucially, the limitations of his power. The king was the vice-gerent on earth of the invisible King, and could be no arbitrary despot unless he plainly transgressed this written charter — a law, Ellicott adds, strictly based upon what Moses had already written (Deut. 17:14–20). John Wesley stands with him, calling it the laws and rules by which the kingly government was to be managed, agreeable to Deut. 17:16, and laid up before the Lord, before the ark, safe from corruption. The distinction matters, and echoes Keil's earlier point that the right of the monarchy is not the despot's prerogative but the ordinance that binds the king under God.

Ellicott draws a further, striking observation on which the others are silent: here, in Samuel's writing about the kingdom, is the first trace of literary composition among the Israelites since the days of Moses, and the great revival of letters that began shortly after Saul was most probably due to Samuel's influence and the schools of the prophets he had founded. He notes too that even after the people had ratified Saul's election, it is still Samuel who dismisses the assembly — throughout his life the seer remains the principal figure, above king and priest. So the witnesses leave the verse holding a real tension: whether the book chiefly warned Israel of a despot's power or chiefly bound the king beneath a constitution, all agree that Samuel meant to fix the landmarks between prince and people, and to lodge the record before the Lord, that the monarchy might never forget under whose authority it stood.
26:
Saul goes home to Gibeah, and a band of men whose hearts God had touched go with him; and the witnesses read the scene for Saul's modesty and for the nature of this escort. John Gill marks the humility of the return: Saul goes to his native place, his father's house, where there was no royal palace, no princely court, none of the ensigns of kingly majesty, and where the nobility did not follow him, only a few men. Charles Ellicott adds that his fellow-citizens, proud of the honour conferred on one of themselves, were doubtless among his earliest devoted attendants, and among these early friends were probably the distinguished men later found around Saul; he reads the highest prudence and sagacity in this quiet beginning, the new king slow to take offence and busy conciliating the disaffected against the day of action soon to come.

The one small divergence is over the band itself. Adam Clarke takes it to be not a military body but some secret friends or companions personally attached to Saul; he weighs the alternative that all the men fit to bear arms are meant, but rejects it as inconsistent with the retired agricultural life Saul led for some time afterward (1 Sam. 11:5). John Wesley agrees that the band went to give the new king safe and honourable conduct to his house, though not to abide with him there, which did not suit his present private circumstances.

All the witnesses fasten on the decisive clause — whose hearts God had touched — and on it their reading converges into the chapter's most quoted lesson, drawn out most fully by Matthew Henry earlier in these verses: whatever good is in us or done by us must be ascribed to the grace of God; if the heart bends at any time the right way, it is because he has touched it, and one touch is enough when it is divine. The loyalty that gathers around Saul is not the natural allegiance of ambition or interest but a thing God worked in men's hearts; and the witnesses hear in it a quiet foreshadowing of the greater King, around whom a remnant likewise rallies because grace has made them willing — over against the despisers v.27 will name.
27:
Some worthless men despise the new king, ask how shall this man save us?, bring him no present, and Saul holds his peace — and the witnesses read the verse for the malcontents, their insult, and the king's magnanimous silence. John Gill takes the children of Belial as men without a yoke, unwilling to be governed, perhaps men of wealth from larger tribes who envied one so obscure. Charles Ellicott sharpens the guess: they were probably princes and leading men of the great tribes of Judah and Ephraim, displeased that the choice had fallen on the small and lately shattered tribe of Benjamin, and they despised Saul because in no way had he yet made his mark in war or peace. Keil and Delitzsch note that the refusal to bring the customary present, the minchah that everyone was expected to offer on entering a king's presence, was almost equivalent to rebellion.

Adam Clarke develops the custom: as Arab chiefs are supplied to this day by the free-will offerings of the people where they encamp, so Saul, now a public character, had a right to public support, and these men refused to bear their part; probably tribute is meant, which the malcontents would not pay on the pretext that such a man could be no deliverer. Yet all the witnesses dwell most on the closing clause. Saul held his peace — literally, Ellicott and Keil observe, he was as a deaf man, acting as though he had not heard the murmurs. Ellicott and Clarke read in this prudent conduct great self-control and self-denial in the new king and his counsellors, a restraint that would let him win the disaffected rather than crush them.

John Wesley gives the verse its final, Christward turn, joining a note that Matthew Henry sounds as well. Saul's silence was evidence of his humility and of the mercifulness of his disposition; and, says Wesley, so Christ held his peace in the day of his patience, but there is a day of recompense coming. The witnesses do not divide; together they make this last scene a mirror of the whole reception of a king. As Israel was split between the loyal band whose hearts God had touched and the scoffers who asked how shall this man save us?, so, the commentators hear, men are divided still before the exalted Redeemer whom God has set as King — a remnant who submit and bring their homage, and others who despise him and stumble at his lowliness. Saul's meek silence under contempt is the human shadow; the patient, kingly silence of Christ, awaiting his day of recompense, is the substance.
